topics.title: Why does the sky appear blue?
prerequisites: White sunlight contains all colors of the rainbow
Different colors of light have different wavelengths (violet shortest, red longest)
learning_objectives: Understand that sunlight scatters when it hits gas molecules in the atmosphere
Recognize that shorter wavelengths (blue/violet) scatter more than longer ones
Explain why the sky looks red/orange at sunset
common_misconceptions: The sky is blue because it reflects the ocean
The atmosphere absorbs the non-blue colors
All colors scatter equally but our eyes prefer blue
socratic_path: What do you know about what sunlight is made of?
If sunlight contains all colors, why do we only see blue up above?
What do you think happens when light hits a tiny particle like a gas molecule?
Which colors of light have shorter wavelengths — closer to violet or closer to red?
If shorter wavelengths scatter more, what color would dominate the scattered light we see?

topics.title: How do plants make food from sunlight?
prerequisites: Plants are living organisms that need energy
Carbon dioxide is a gas in the air
Glucose is a type of sugar used for energy
learning_objectives: Identify the inputs (CO2, water, light) and outputs (glucose, oxygen) of photosynthesis
Understand chlorophyll's role in capturing light energy
Recognize that plants are the base of most food chains
common_misconceptions: Plants get their food from soil
The oxygen plants release is from water, not CO2
socratic_path: Where do you think a plant gets the material to grow bigger?
A plant can grow in just water and light with no soil — what does that tell you?
If a plant converts light energy into stored energy, what molecule might store that energy?
What gas do plants breathe in, and what do they breathe out?

topics.title: Why do objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass?
prerequisites: Gravity exerts a force on all objects with mass
Force equals mass times acceleration (F = ma)
Heavier objects experience a greater gravitational force
learning_objectives: Understand why greater gravitational force on a heavier object is exactly cancelled by its greater inertia
Distinguish between weight (force) and mass (resistance to acceleration)
common_misconceptions: Heavier objects fall faster because gravity pulls harder on them
A feather falls slower because it is lighter (ignoring air resistance)
Mass and weight are the same thing
socratic_path: If gravity pulls harder on heavier objects, why do they not fall faster?
What does Newton's F = ma tell us about how force, mass, and acceleration relate?
If a 10 kg ball experiences 10x more gravitational force than a 1 kg ball, what does F = ma predict about their accelerations?
What would happen if you dropped a hammer and a feather on the Moon with no air?

topics.title: Why does carbon dioxide cause global warming?
prerequisites: The Sun emits energy as radiation including visible light
The Earth absorbs sunlight and re-emits it as infrared (heat) radiation
Different gases interact differently with different wavelengths of radiation
learning_objectives: Understand the greenhouse effect as a natural and necessary phenomenon
Explain how CO2 molecules absorb and re-emit infrared radiation
Distinguish the natural greenhouse effect from anthropogenic (human-caused) enhancement
common_misconceptions: The ozone hole causes global warming
CO2 is a pollutant that blocks sunlight from reaching Earth
The greenhouse effect is entirely harmful — without it Earth would be frozen
socratic_path: If the Earth absorbs sunlight, what do you think happens to that energy?
Why does the Earth not just keep heating up from all the sunlight it absorbs every day?
What if there were a gas that absorbed the outgoing heat radiation and sent some of it back down?
If you added more of that gas to the atmosphere, what would happen to Earth's temperature?

topics.title: How does natural selection drive evolution?
prerequisites: Organisms reproduce and pass traits to offspring
Offspring are not identical to parents — there is variation
Resources in the environment are limited
learning_objectives: Understand the four conditions required for natural selection (variation, heritability, differential survival, reproduction)
Explain how environment determines which traits are advantageous
Distinguish natural selection from directed evolution or goal-directed change
common_misconceptions: Organisms evolve because they want or need to
Evolution has a direction or goal (toward complexity or perfection)
Individual organisms evolve during their lifetime
socratic_path: If all offspring were identical to their parents, could a species ever change over time?
Imagine two rabbits — one slightly faster, one slightly slower. What happens when a fox chases them?
If the faster rabbit survives and has babies, what might those babies tend to be like?
Could this process produce a completely new species eventually? What would it take?
